coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots-Nafion as proton selective hybrid membrane for direct methanol fuel cells
coredata.dc:creator: Parthiban, V.
Panda, Subhendu K.
Sahu, Akhila Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we explored the carbon quantum dots as potential inorganic filler to realize a Nafion hybrid membrane with much reduction in methanol crossover for direct methanol fuel cell and is first ever reported in the literature. Highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots is synthesized by simple hydrothermal process using Gelatin as source material and the formation of carbon quantum dots is confirmed by UV–Visible and Photoluminescence spectroscopy studies. The added carbon quantum dots filler promotes the ionic conductivity primarily via hydrogen bonding formation between plenty of hydrophilic groups exist in carbon quantum dots and sulfonic acid moieties in the Nafion matrix. Remarkably, the optimized Nafion-carbon quantum dots hybrid membrane shows about 33% reduction in methanol crossover and about 30% higher proton conductivity in relation to the pristine Nafion membrane. While utilizing in direct methanol fuel cell, a peak power density of 113 mW cm−2 is realized with optimized Nafion-carbon quantum dots hybrid membrane at 70 °C and ambient pressure which is about 75% higher in performance than that of pristine Nafion membrane operated under identical conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hybrid membrane
Carbon quantum dots
Methanol permeability
Fuel cell
Electrochemical selectivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of Nafion- CQDs hybrid membrane
Water uptake and ion-exchange capacity of the membranes
Fabrication of the membrane electrode assemblies (MEAs) and fuel cell performance evaluation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from Gelatin by hydrothermal process.

(a) X-ray diffraction pattern of carbon quantum dots (CQDs); (b) FT-IR spectrum of as-synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs); (c) UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (the inset show the sample under normal and UV lamp radiation) and (d) the fluorescence lifetime decay curve of the CQD in powder form.

TEM images of as-synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) at two different magnification (a) distribution of CQDs at 100 nm (inset shows the particle size distribution); (b) presence of lattice fringes of CQD shown at 10 nm (inset shows the SAED pattern).

(a) EIS of Pristine Nafion and Nafion-CQDs hybrid membrane at 70 °C under 100% relative humidity; (b) Proton conductivity of pristine Nafion and Nafion-CQDs hybrid membrane at different temperatures under 100% relative humidity.

(a) Thermo gravimetric (TG); (b) Derivative thermogravimetric (DTG) analysis; (c) Stress-Strain curve and (d) Small angle x-ray scattering analysis of Pristine Nafion and Nafion-CQDs hybrid membranes.
(a and b) Topography; (c and d) 3D Phase images; (e and f) Line profile of pristine Nafion and Nafion-CQDs (1.5 wt%) hybrid membrane.
(a) Methanol crossover current density; (b) Electrochemical selectivity of Pristine Nafion and Nafion-CQDs hybrid membranes.

(a) DMFCs performance of Pristine Nafion, Nafion-117 and Nafion-CQDs hybrid membranes at 70 °C under ambient pressure; (b) Stability test for pristine Nafion and Nafion-CQDs (1.5 wt %) hybrid membrane at 70 °C under OCV conditions; (c) DMFCs performance of Pristine Nafion and Nafion-CQDs (1.5 wt%) membrane after 50 h stability test at 70 °C under ambient pressure.
Water uptake, Ion-exchange capacity, Peak power density, Methanol crossover current density and Electro-chemical selectivity of Pristine Nafion and Nafion-CQDs hybrid membranes.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots-Nafion as proton selective hybrid membrane for direct methanol fuel cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR - Central Electrochemical Research Institute-Madras Unit, CSIR Madras Complex, Taramani, Chennai, 600113, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR - Central Electrochemical Research Institute-Madras Unit

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR-Central Electrochemical Research Institute, Karaikudi, 630 003, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), CSIR - Central Electrochemical Research Institute, Karaikudi, 630003, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. CSIR - Central Electrochemical Research Institute-Madras Unit, CSIR Madras Complex, Taramani, Chennai, 600113, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR - Central Electrochemical Research Institute-Madras Unit

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we explored the carbon quantum dots as potential inorganic filler to realize a Nafion hybrid membrane with much reduction in methanol crossover for direct methanol fuel cell and is first ever reported in the literature. Highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots is synthesized by simple hydrothermal process using Gelatin as source material and the formation of carbon quantum dots is confirmed by UV–Visible and Photoluminescence spectroscopy studies. The added carbon quantum dots filler promotes the ionic conductivity primarily via hydrogen bonding formation between plenty of hydrophilic groups exist in carbon quantum dots and sulfonic acid moieties in the Nafion matrix. Remarkably, the optimized Nafion-carbon quantum dots hybrid membrane shows about 33% reduction in methanol crossover and about 30% higher proton conductivity in relation to the pristine Nafion membrane. While utilizing in direct methanol fuel cell, a peak power density of 113 mW cm−2 is realized with optimized Nafion-carbon quantum dots hybrid membrane at 70 °C and ambient pressure which is about 75% higher in performance than that of pristine Nafion membrane operated under identical conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: Direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs), owing to its high specific energy density, have been widely appealed as a substitute to Li-ion battery in powering most of the portable and stationary electronic applications such as mobile phone, laptops etc. [1–3]. Convenient storage and handling of methanol offers simplicity and portability to DMFC system which also avoids auxiliary units such as humidifier and fuel reformer which are essential in H2 based fuel cells [4]. However, the methanol crossover across the polymer electrolyte membrane (PEM), CO poisoning, poor electrode kinetics for both methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) and oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) hinders the commercialization of DMFC [5]. Particularly, the methanol crossover affects the DMFC performance loss by creating a mixed potential at the cathode, poisoning the cathode catalyst and reducing the fuel availability on the anode [6]. To improve overall DMFC performance, it is essential to minimize the methanol crossover by developing alternate cost effective PEM materials with low methanol permeability, high proton conductivity and stability.

In this context, many researchers have proposed various PEM materials as potential candidates to reduce methanol crossover which includes acid-base composites, modification of perfluorinated membranes, non-fluorinated membranes such as sulfonated poly (ether ether ketone) (sPEEK), poly vinyl alcohol (PVA), sulfonated poly (phenylene oxide) (SPPO) and their composites [7,8]. Amongst, Nafion composite membranes is superior in achieving enhanced DMFC performance owing to its high proton conductivity and excellent stability of base polymer Nafion. Generally, Nafion membrane is composed of crystalline phase of poly tetrafluoro ethylene (PTFE) backbone which offers the stability and amorphous phase of perfluoro ether side chain ended with sulfonic acid moieties provides the proton conductivity [9,10]. In a DMFC, the methanol can travel from anode to cathode across Nafion membrane via the same pathway of proton through electro-osmotic drag and widened water channels (∼2.5 nm) which limit the DMFC performance [2,11].

Among various strategies proposed to modify the Nafion membrane towards reduced methanol crossover, the inclusion of nano-sized inorganic fillers to the Nafion matrix paid grate attention due to the specific interaction between inorganic filler and Nafion matrix leads to improve the mechanical stability. The problem associated on phase separation between fillers and polymer matrix also would be overcome significantly by using either surface functionalized inorganic filler prior to its impregnation to polymer matrix or by adopting in-situ sol-gel process to incorporate hydroxide form of filler materials [1,12]. The functionalized fillers alter the size of ionic/water transport channels and forms the long range continuous ionic clusters which specifically encourage proton transport in the composite membrane. Till date, inorganic materials such as SiO2 [13,14], TiO2 [15], zirconium phosphate [6], Heteropoly acids [16], porous carbon [17], carbon nanotube (CNT) [18] and 2D materials including the functionalized graphene [19], graphitic carbon nitride [20] graphene oxides [21–23], boron nitride nanosheets [9] has been reported as effective fillers to Nafion matrix and the hybrid membrane prepared from them shows drastic reduction in the methanol crossover from anode to the cathode. Hydrophilic groups of surface functionalized filler enhance overall proton conductivity and water retention properties of the hybrid membranes. Combination of reduced methanol crossover and improved proton conductivity resulted in enhancing the DMFC performance.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is an emerging nano material with unique optical, electronic and structural features associated with abundant hydrophilic groups (COOH, OH etc.) is being explored in many advance technologies such as electro and photo catalysis, bio-imaging, sensors and biomedicine applications [24,25]. The size and the functionalities of CQDs can be controlled by adopting different synthesis routes which allow us to alter its physicochemical properties for a wide range of applications. Recently, W. Jia et al. fabricated the Nafion composite membrane by incorporating Nafion modified carbon dots (NCD) for fuel cell applications. They claimed that the enhancement in the proton conductivity is due to the multifunctional groups (COOH, OH from carbon dots and SO3H from Nafion) present in NCD filler [26]. However, very few literatures available in investigating the precise contribution of CQDs and its study in enhancing physico-chemical properties of the PEM especially for fuel cell applications. We believed that the nano-sized CQDs with weak acidic group such as COOH and other hydrophilic functional groups will provide the additional proton conducting pathway and good connectivity between the hydrophilic domains when used in modifying the Nafion membrane.

In the present study, CQDs with abundant functional groups was prepared by a simple one step hydrothermal method using Gelatin as source material. Highly fluorescent behaviour of synthesized CQD is ascertained with UV–Visible and photoluminescence spectroscopy studies. CQDs in suspension form provide homogeneous dispersion between fillers and Nafion and improve the compatibility of hybrid membranes during membrane casting. The abundant hydrophilic groups in CQDs further ensure the formation of continues ionic channels for facile proton transport. Physicochemical properties of the hybrid membrane such as water uptake, proton conductivity and methanol crossover are contrasted with single cell DMFC performance and found potential of CQDs in designing a superior hybrid membrane which further boosts the DMFC performance.

Gelatin with 99.99% purity was procured from Merck, Germany and the Nafion5 wt% ionomer was obtained from DuPont, USA. The catalyst Pt/C (40 wt% Pt on Vulcan XC-72R carbon) and Pt-Ru/C(40 wt% Pt and 20 wt% Ru on Vulcan XC-72R carbon) was obtained from Alfa Aesar (Johnson Matthey) Ltd. The Gas diffusion layers (Sigracet DC-35 GDL) were obtained from SGL group, Germany. All the chemicals were used as received and Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩ cm) is used throughout the experiments.

The CQDs was prepared by a simple one step hydrothermal process similar to the process described elsewhere [27]. In brief, 1.8 g of Gelatin was mechanically mixed in 80 mL of water and the resultant solution was transferred to the 100 mL stainless steel autoclave with Teflon container inside. The autoclave was placed in a muffle furnace at 200 °C for 3 h for hydrothermal process. After cooling down to room temperature, the light yellow colour solution was obtained which is centrifuged at 4000 rpm to separate and sediment the large size particles. After centrifugation process, the light yellow colour changes to the light brown which is labelled as CQDs dispersion and used to fabricate Nafion hybrid membrane and subjected to further characterizations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of Nafion- CQDs hybrid membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The hybrid membrane was fabricated by well-known solution casting method. The specific amount of CQDs suspension was added to the required volume of 5 wt % of Nafion ionomer and ultrasonically dispersed for an hour followed by mechanical mixing for 4 h. The hybrid membrane was obtained by casting the resultant admixture in a glass petri-dish and subsequent vacuum drying at 80 °C for 8 h. After cooling, the membrane was peeled off from the dish by adding water. Prior to use, all the membranes was pre-treated in 0.5 M sulphuric acid at 80 °C for 1 h and then washed with the plenty of water until the pH reaches neutral. The thickness of the membrane was measured at 5 random points and the average thickness of all the membrane is ∼170 μm. The pristine Nafion membrane is also prepared without any filler in the same way as mentioned above and used for the comparison.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), BRUKER D8 advance diffractometer using Cu-Kα (λ = 1.5 Å) radiation. Morphology and size of synthesized CQDs was observed by High resolution - transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM FEI Tecnai F20). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded in the frequency range 400–4000 cm−1 by Bruker TENSOR 27 infrared spectrophotometer using KBr pellets sample matrix. The thermal stability of pristine Nafion and Nafion-CQDs membranes was evaluated using NETZSCH STA 449TG-DSC instrument in the temperature range between 30 and 900 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 under N2 atmosphere. Small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) profiles were recorded to understand the microstructure of the membranes on a Rigaku smart lab guidance diffractometer with a Cu-Kα (λ = 1.541 Å) radiation operating at 45 kV, 200 mA. The microstructural changes in the Nafion-CQDs hybrid membrane was studied using atomic force microscopy (AFM) (AFM, Pico SPM-Picoscan 2100, Molecular Imaging, USA) in contact mode with a diamond-like carbon coated ultra-sharp silicon tip.

Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were performed at room temperature using a Horiba JobinYvon FluoroLog spectrofluorometer (Instruments SA). Luminescence lifetime decays were measured by time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) with a DeltaHub system attached to the FluoroLog instrument. A Nano LED of 390 nm excitation wavelength was employed as the excitation source and a pulse repetition rate of 500 kHz was employed for the lifetime measurements. The instrument response function (IRF) was measured using a Ludox scattering solution in water and the decay curves were fitted to a bi-exponential function using the proprietary software Data Station 6, where the average reduced weighted chi-squared residual (χ2) value is < 1.2. The mean fluorescence lifetimes (τ) for the bi-exponential iterative fitting were calculated from the decay time and the pre-exponential factors using the following equation, where τi is the lifetime of a component having relative amplitude αi.
(1)
τ
average
=
∑
i = 1
n
α
i
τ
i
2
∑
i = 1
n
α
i
τ
i

The in-plane proton conductivity of the membrane samples at different temperatures was evaluated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using Bekktech four-probe conductivity cell (BT-112). The membrane samples with active area of 4 cm2 was placed in contact with platinum electrodes. The requisite temperature and humidity was controlled by humidity chamber (Espec, SH-242). The potentiostat (Biologic, SP-150) was used to record the ac impedance spectra in the frequency range between 1 MHz and 1 Hz with amplitude of 10 mV. EIS was recorded after fully conditioning of the cell at the particular temperature and humidity. The resistance value obtained in the x-axis intercept at high frequency region (HFR) considered for the proton conductivity calculation using following equation;
(2)
σ =
L
R × W × T
where,σ is the proton conductivity of the membrane in S cm−1; L = 0.425 cm, the fixed distance between two Pt electrodes; R is the membrane resistance in Ω; W is the width of the sample in cm; and T is the thickness of the membrane in cm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water uptake and ion-exchange capacity of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
To measure the water uptake capacity, a piece of membrane samples was pre-dried at 80 °C for 6 h and weighed to get the dry weight of the membrane (Wd). Subsequently, the dried membrane sample was drenched in a water filled glass container for one day. Then, the hydrated sample was removed from the container and surplus surface water was carefully removed using tissue paper and weighed to obtain wet weight of the membrane (Ww). From the difference between wet and dry weight, the water uptake was calculated using equation (3);
(3)
Water
uptake
(
%
)
=
W
w
−
W
d
W
d
× 100
where, Wd and Ww are the weight of wet and dry membranes, respectively.

To quantify the Ion-exchange Capacity (IEC), a 0.5 g of the sample was soaked in 30 mL of 3 N NaCl for 24 h to exchange H+ with Na+ ions and the displaced H+ ions in sample was measured by the acid-base titration method. The known volume of NaCl solution titrated against 0.01 N NaOH solution using phenolphthalein as an indicator. IEC of the sample was calculated using the equation (4).
(4)
IEC =
Volumeof
NaOH
consumed
×
Molarity
of
NaOH
Weight
of
the
dried
sample
mmol / g

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the membrane electrode assemblies (MEAs) and fuel cell performance evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: Commercial gas diffusion layer (GDL, DC-35) was used to fabricate the anode and the cathode electrode. To constitute the anode electrode, specific amount of Pt-Ru/C (Pt-40 wt % and Ru-20 wt %) catalyst was ultrasonically dispersed in isopropyl alcohol with 10 wt% Nafion binder to obtain the homogeneous catalyst slurry. The resultant slurry was uniformly applied on the GDL to reach the catalyst loading of 2 mg cm−2. The same procedure was followed with the 40 wt % Pt/C catalyst and 30 wt % Nafion binder to fabricate the cathode electrode with the catalyst loading of 2 mg cm−2. The MEAs were fabricated by hot pressing the pre-treated membranes sandwiched between the anode and cathode electrodes at 130 °C under 10 kg cm−2 for 3 min.

The DMFC performance of MEAs was evaluated in a single fuel cell fixture with the active area of 4 cm2 obtained from Fuel cell technologies Inc. USA. 2 M aqueous methanol solution (flow rate is 2 mL min−1) and oxygen (flow rate is 250 mL min−1) were supplied as the fuel and oxidant on anode and cathode inlet of the DMFC, respectively. The DMFC performance of all the hybrid membrane was evaluated at 70 °C under ambient pressure. DMFC performance evaluation was also done with the pristine Nafion membrane under similar operating conditions for comparison.

Methanol crossover of the pristine Nafion and Nafion-CQDs membranes was measured by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) in cell mode using potentiostat/galvanostat (Biologic, VSP/VMP 3B-20) at room temperature. LSV was recorded from 0 to 1.0 V at the scan rate of 2 mV s−1. The individual MEAs fabricated with the pristine and hybrid membranes were assembled in a DMFC single cell (active area is 4 cm2) and conditioned for one hour at open circuit voltage (OCV). The oxygen was replaced by nitrogen on the cathode at a flow rate of 100 mL min−1 and methanol was supplied at a flow rate of 2 mL min−1 during the measurements. The working electrode was connected to the cathode of the cell while counter and reference electrodes were connected to the anode of the cell. Methanol diffuses from the anode to the cathode and get oxidized at the cathode which produces the current. The resultant current known as methanol cross-over current which gradually increases as voltage sweep from 0 to 1 V and reaches the plateau after particular voltage when the diffused methanol get completely oxidized in the steady state condition.

Fig. 1 exemplify the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from gelatin by hydrothermal process and blue emission of as synthesized CQDs suspension under UV light. A small amount of CQDs suspension was dried under the vacuum and the resultant powder was used for XRD analysis. The X-ray pattern presented in Fig. 2 a shows a broad diffraction peak at 2Θ ∼22° corresponding to the graphitic (002) plane indicating the highly amorphous nature of CQD. In addition, the larger (002) interlayer spacing of CQDs (4.0 Å) compared to that of bulk graphite (d = 3.3 Å) also evident the amorphous nature of CQDs. The amorphous characteristic arises from the presence of plentiful functional groups in CQDs [28].

FT-IR spectra was recorded to get the information on the functional groups present in as-synthesized CQDs and the corresponding spectra shown in Fig. 2b. The FT-IR peaks near 1400 cm−1 corresponding to the aromatic CC stretching vibrations conform the presence of sp2 hybridized graphitic network [29]. Two peaks at 2960 and 1300 cm−1 is associated with the stretching and bending vibration of CH bond. The peaks at around 3300, 1110 and 1660 cm−1 corresponding to the OH stretching, CO stretching and CO stretching vibrations confirms the presence of alcoholic and carboxylic functional groups in the prepared CQDs [30]. In addition, the presence of amino-containing functional groups can be conformed from the peaks corresponding to the stretching and vibrations of NH bond at around 3100 and 1550 cm−1 [31]. The presence of more hydrophilic functional groups is expected to boost up the proton conductivity by forming the additional proton conductivity pathway. Additionally, the strong interaction between the CQDs functional groups and the hydrophilic domain of Nafion membranes is anticipated to increase the membrane stability.

As seen in Fig. 2c, the UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy of CQDs exhibit an absorption peak at around 230 nm corresponding to the π−π∗ transition of aromatic CC bond, suggesting the formation of CQDs with more aromatic sp2 domain. Two more absorption peaks in the wavelength range of 260–360 nm is corresponding to the n−π∗ of CO bond [32,33]. In the photoluminescence studies, a broad emission spectrum centred at 485 nm was observed with an excitation wavelength at 300 nm. By varying the excitation wavelength there is not much shift in the peak position is observed. The emission may be ascribed to the abundant surface defects present in the synthesized CQDs. During the synthesis, the surface of the CQDs partially oxidizes to form functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl moieties on the surface, which makes the CQDs hydrophilic and negatively charged [34]. The trap states on the surface of CQDs formed due to the doping of oxygen into the carbon nanoparticles exhibit strong fluorescence [27]. The inset images in Fig. 2c represents the images of the powder samples under ambient light and UV lamp of excitation wavelength 365 nm. The CQDs show bright white colour under the UV lamp. Fig. 2d illustrates the time-resolved fluorescence decay curve measured by TCSPC method and the decay curve can be very well fitted to a bi-exponential function. From the fitted plot the calculated decay lifetimes are τ1 = 1.87 and τ2 = 6.80 ns with relative amplitude of α1 = 65.52 and α2 = 34.48 respectively. The mean lifetime is calculated to be 5.11 ns, which indicate the presence of different decay pathways for electron-hole recombination from the core and surface states generated from photo excitation.

Fig. 3 a and b shows the HR-TEM morphology and size distribution of the CQDs at two different magnifications. HR-TEM images revealed that the CQDs is in spherical shape and dots are well separated. The average particle size was measured using imageJ image processing software and found to be around 2 nm with the standard deviation of 0.5 nm (inset to Fig. 3a). The presence of lattice fringes with the inter-planar spacing of about 0.24 nm which is close to d spacing of (100) plane of sp2 graphitic carbon indicating the crystalline nature of CQDs [Fig. 3b] [35]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 3b inset) shows sharper ring with few dots indicating the polycrystalline nature of the CQDs with a preferential orientation. The outer diffused ring may be due to the presence of amorphous gelatine matrix.

After successful preparation and characterization of CQDs, the required amount of CQDs dispersion was added to the specific amount of 5 wt% Nafion ionomer to realize the Nafion-CQDs hybrid membrane with different weight percentage of CQDs. The water uptake capacity (WUC) of any proton conducting membrane is a key factor as it directly impacts the proton conductivity and mechanical stability. The water uptake capacity of pristine Nafion and Nafion-CQDs hybrid membrane was quantified from the weight difference between wet and dry membrane and the corresponding results shown in Table 1 . The WUC of all the hybrid membrane is relatively higher than that of pristine Nafion and gradually increases as increases the content of CQDs in Nafion matrix up to 1.5 wt%. The higher WUC arises due to the presence of abundant hydrophilic functional groups present in the CQDs. The higher WUC will positively alters the Nafion microstructure by increasing the size of the ionic clusters which helps in boosting up the proton conductivity by vehicular mechanism.

Ion exchange capacity (IEC) measures the number of labile protons per gram of the sample and is an important parameter to understand the membrane proton conductivity. As can be seen in Table 1, the IEC of Nafion-CQDs membranes are higher compared to pristine Nafion membrane. The Nafion-CQDs membrane (1.5 wt%) showed the higher IEC (1.02 mmol g−1) among the hybrid membrane which is about 13% higher than that of pristine Nafion membrane (0.90 mmol g−1). The increased IEC arises due to the abundant functional groups such as COOH, OH present in CQDs which releases the additional protons as in the case of GO/Nafion composite membrane [36]. As observed in many hybrid membranes, lower water intake capacity and IEC was observed for the Nafion-CQDs membrane with 2.0 wt % filler content may be attributed to the minor agglomeration of the fillers in the Nafion microstructure.

Proton conductivity of the PEM is a most important property as it directly affects the overall fuel cell performance. The AC electrochemical impedance spectroscopy analysis was employed to measure the proton conductivity of pristine Nafion and Nafion-CQDs hybrid membrane. EIS was recorded at different temperatures in the frequency range between 1 MHz and 1 Hz under fully humidified conditions. Fig. 4 a shows the impedance spectra recorded at 70 °C under 100% RH. The Nafion-CQDs (1.5 wt %) hybrid membrane exhibit the resistance of (274.2 Ω cm2) is very less than that of pristine Nafion membrane (338.7 Ω cm2). The lower resistance of the hybrid membrane can be understood from the improved water uptake capacity and IEC. As the temperature has the direct impacts on the activation energy of proton conduction, the proton conductivity was calculated at different temperature and displayed in Fig. 4b. Proton conductivity increases as the function of temperature up to 90 °C for all the membranes and drops at 100 °C. For the Nafion-CQDs (1.5 wt %) membrane, the proton conductivity of 85.8 and 180.6 mS cm−1 was achieved at 30 °C and 70 °C, respectively, which is about 40 and 30.0% higher compared to that of pristine Nafion. The higher proton conductivity of hybrid membrane mainly arises from the contribution of both Grötthuss and vehicular type of proton conduction. The higher IEC of hybrid membrane evidence the additional labile proton is available for the Grötthuss type of mechanism in which proton is transported via the hydrogen bonding formation [37,38]. In addition, some of the proton can form the hydronium ion with the bound water molecules and transported via vehicular molecules as a result of higher water uptake capacity of the hybrid membrane. All the Nafion-CQDs membrane displayed higher proton conductivity over a temperature range.

Thermal stability of the pristine Nafion and Nafion-CQDs hybrid membrane was measured by TGA analysis and the corresponding results is presented in Fig. 5 a. The initial weight loss up to 200 °C of all the membrane is due to the loss of surface and bound water present in the membrane. As CQDs contains more functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxylic acid etc., the Nafion hybrid membrane fabricated with CQDs filler is expected to be more hydrophilic and prevents the bound water loss compared to pristine Nafion membrane. The first stage weight loss in the temperature range of 260–380 °C is mainly due to the loss of sulfonic acid moieties from the Nafion side chains and the second weight loss between 380 and 450 °C is mainly corresponding to the degradation of perfluorovinyl ether side chains. A very sharp decrease after 450 °C is observed because of the polytetrafluoro ethylene (PTFE) backbone degradation of the membrane [39,40].

As seen in Fig. 5b, the DTG analysis clearly exemplify the increase in membrane thermal stability of all the Nafion hybrid membranes. Noticeably, the peak value of 1st, 2nd and 3rd stage degradation is shifted to higher temperature about 5, 17 and 5 °C for Nafion-CQDs membrane with 2 wt% filler content in relation to the pristine Nafion membrane. The enhancement in thermal stability of Nafion-CQDs membranes is arises from the strong hydrogen bonding interaction between the sulfonic acid groups of Nafion and the hydrophilic functional groups of CQDs. The improved thermal stability of Nafion-CQDs hybrid membrane is also credited to the nano sized CQDs filled with unique functional groups responsible for compact Nafion microstructure without introducing much strain to the original polymer matrix.

The mechanical stability of Nafion-CQD hybrid membrane in comparison with the pristine recast Nafion membrane is displayed in Fig. 5c. The incorporation of CQDs into the Nafion matrix shows the improved mechanical stability with the tensile strength and elongation of 8.0 MPa and 20%, respectively. On the other hand, the pristine Nafion membrane exhibited tensile strength and elongation of only 5.0 MPa and 8.5% elongation, respectively. About 60% increase in tensile strength for Nafion-CQDs hybrid membrane mainly attributed to the reduced phase separation due to the strong hydrogen bonding interaction between the filler and polymer matrix. The changes in the Nafion microstructure is evaluated by Small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) as shown in Fig. 5d. The ionomer peak of Nafion membrane is usually seen between q (scattering vector) of ∼0.11 and ∼0.14 Å−1 and the peak corresponding to fluorocarbon crystallites is seen at ∼0.05 Å−1, known as “matrix knee”. In our study, the pristine recast Nafion membrane exhibit the ionomer peak and matrix knee peak at about ∼0.14 Å−1 and ∼0.05 Å−1, respectively. In the case of Nafion-CQD hybrid membrane, the ionomer peak shifted to a lower q value (0.12 Å−1) with no obvious change in matrix knee peak position. This suggests the increase in ionic cluster size from ∼4.5 nm for pristine Nafion to ∼5.2 nm for Nafion-CQDs hybrid membrane. The Nafion hybrid membrane with increased ionic cluster size is expected to contribute in facile proton transport.

The changes in the micro-structure of Nafion matrix after incorporation of CQDs was also observed by AFM analysis and compared with the pristine Nafion membrane. Fig. 6 shows the corresponding AFM micrographs of Nafion-CQDs (1.5 wt%) and pristine Nafion membrane. The AFM analysis was performed at a scan area of 5 μm × 5 μm. As seen in topography and 3D phase images of pristine Nafion membrane (Fig. 6 a and c), the hydrophilic channels are well-separated and narrowed. In contrast, the Nafion-CQDs membrane possesses relatively widened hydrophilic channels due to the presence of hydrophilic CQDs (Fig. 6 b and d). Unlike other inorganic fillers, the addition of CQDs is not altering the surface roughness of the membrane as seen in surface roughness profile of pristine Nafion and Nafion-CQDs membrane shown in Fig. 6 e and f. The route-mean squared (RMS) value obtained for pristine Nafion and Nafion-CQDs membrane is 2.57 and 2.53 nm, respectively. This is mainly because of the nanosize of CQDs which can be accommodate in the Nafion microstructure without making strain.

Methanol permeability through Nafion membrane from the anode to the cathode electrode is awfully affects the overall DMFCs efficiency by reducing the fuel efficiency and poisoning the cathode catalyst. We have characterized the methanol crossover behaviour of Nafion-CQDs hybrid membrane at room temperature by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) technique (Fig. 7 a).

All the hybrid membrane showed a lower methanol crossover limiting current density than pristine Nafion membrane. Among the different composition of hybrid membrane Nafion-CQDs (2.0 wt%) possess the lowest methanol crossover limiting current density of 107 mA cm−2 which is 36% lesser compared to pristine Nafion (168 mA cm−2). The improved methanol resistive effect of hybrid membrane is primarily arises from the strong interaction between the hydrophilic groups present in the CQDs and sulfonic acid moieties of the Nafion membrane. This alters the water channel size and reduces the free volume in the hydrophilic domain of the Nafion membrane, hence methanol transport by both electro osmotic drag and concentration gradient is minimized [41].

The membrane selectivity is one of the important parameter to evaluate the overall electrochemical properties of the membrane. This can be done by taking the ratio between proton conductivity and the methanol permeability. A membrane for DMFC applications should possess the higher proton conductivity and lower methanol permeability, hence higher selectivity. The selectivity of the Nafion-CQDs membranes and pristine Nafion membrane was calculated from the membrane resistance and the methanol crossover limiting current density using the following equation [42];
(5)
s =
1
R
s
×
I
c r o s s − o v e r
S m
A
− 1
where, S is the electrochemical selectivity (in S mA−1); Icross-over is the Methanol crossover limiting current density (in mA cm−1); Rs area-specific resistance (in ohm cm2). The area-specific resistance is calculated by the following equation;
(6)
R
s
=
t
σ
o h m
c
m
2
where, t is the membrane thickness (in cm); σ is the proton conductivity (in S cm−1).

As seen in Fig. 7b and Table 1, the Nafion-CQDs (1.5 wt %) hybrid membrane showed higher selectivity among all the membrane and it is nearly 115% higher than that of pristine Nafion membrane. Although Nafion-CQDs (2.0 wt%) showed slightly higher methanol barrier effect than Nafion-CQDs (1.5 wt %) membrane, the lower selectivity of that membrane can be understood from its lower proton conductivity. Combined effect of low methanol permeability and higher proton conductivity of Nafion-CQDs (1.5 wt %) hybrid membrane expose the optimal filler content in the Nafion matrix.

The practical application of Nafion-CQDs hybrid membranes was studied in single cell DMFC and compared with pristine Nafion membrane as shown in Fig. 8 a. All the hybrid membrane exhibits the better DMFC performance than pristine Nafion membrane. Particularly, the Nafion-CQDs (1.5 wt %) hybrid membrane delivered a peak power density of 113 mW cm−2 with the load current density of 431 mA cm−2 at 70 °C under ambient pressure. This is about 75% and 64% higher in DMFC performance than that of pristine Nafion membrane (65 mW cm−2) and Nafion-117 membrane (69 mW cm−2) under similar operating conditions. Moreover, DMFC comprising Nafion-CQD hybrid membranes showed much higher open circuit voltage (OCV) in relation to pristine Nafion membrane which further indicates the reduction in methanol crossover and no significant adverse effect of electronic conductivity in the hybrid membranes. Higher DMFC performance of Nafion-CQD membranes is finally credited to their higher electrochemical selectivity as discussed in previous sections. Stability of Nafion-CQDs (1.5 wt%) hybrid membrane is also evaluated by monitoring the OCV for 50 h as depicted in Fig. 8b and compared with pristine Nafion membrane. Interestingly, DMFCs employed with Nafion-CQDs (1.5 wt%) hybrid membrane showed only about 9% loss in OCV which is relatively low compared to the 15% OCV drop observed for DMFCs employed with pristine Nafion membrane under similar operating conditions. As presented in Fig. 8c, the DMFC employed Nafion-CQDs (1.5 wt %) membrane retained about 87% (98 mW cm−2) of its initial peak power density after 50 h stability test while the pristine recast Nafion membrane retained only about 54% (35 mW cm−2) under identical operating conditions. The performance reduction is mainly due to methanol crossover from anode to cathode electrode which gets oxidize on the cathode forming mixed potential and poisoning the cathode catalyst. However, low methanol crossover of Nafion-CQDs composite membrane compared to pristine Nafion membrane enables to retain its higher DMFC performance even after 50 h of stability test. By considering the higher DMFC performance and stability, the Nafion-CQDs membrane presented in this study can be considered as the potential candidate in the progress of developing an alternate PEM material for DMFC applications.

Fluorescent CQDs is successfully synthesized by a simple hydrothermal process and the fluorescent behaviour is confirmed with wide range of absorption in PL spectra. The as-synthesized CQDs with abundant hydrophilic functional groups is responsible in realizing the novel Nafion hybrid membrane for DMFCs applications. Characteristically, Nafion-CQDs hybrid membrane exhibits proton conductivity of 180 mS cm−1 with reduced methanol crossover current density compared to pristine Nafion membrane. Consequently, the single cell DMFC assembled with the Nafion-CQDs (1.5 wt %) delivered the maximum peak power density of 113 mW cm−2 which is about 75% higher than that of pristine Nafion membrane (65 mW cm−2). The enhanced performance of Nafion-CQDs membrane with marked stability signifies the prospective of these hybrid membranes in DMFC technology.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support by CSIR, New Delhi, is gratefully acknowledged. We thank Dr.Vijayamohanan K. Pillai, Director, CECRI, Karaikudi, for his constant encouragement and support. V.P thanks CSIR for awarding Senior Research Fellowship (31/20 (158)/2017-EMR-I) to pursue research at CSIR-CECRI.
